source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
Suppose \( x_1(t) \) and \( x_2(t) \) have the Fourier transforms as shown in the <image>.

Which one of the following statements is TRUE?
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text: \( x_1(t) \) and \( x_2(t) \) are complex but \( x_1(t)x_2(t) \) is real

choice_map.A: \( x_1(t) \) and \( x_2(t) \) are complex and \( x_1(t)x_2(t) \) is also complex with nonzero imaginary part
choice_map.B: \( x_1(t) \) and \( x_2(t) \) are real and \( x_1(t)x_2(t) \) is also real
choice_map.C: \( x_1(t) \) and \( x_2(t) \) are complex but \( x_1(t)x_2(t) \) is real
choice_map.D: \( x_1(t) \) and \( x_2(t) \) are imaginary but \( x_1(t)x_2(t) \) is real

extraction_notes: image field is a PIL image object representation, not a path or URL
solution field contains only option letter and was mapped using extracted choices

issues: Question asks 'Which one of the following statements is TRUE?' but no choice options are provided in the input.

image_dependency: question_text explicitly says the Fourier transforms are 'as shown in the figure'
the truth of the options depends on visual properties/symmetry of the shown transforms, which are not recoverable from text alone
multi_step: solving requires interpreting each displayed Fourier transform first, then inferring whether x1(t) and x2(t) are real/complex/imaginary
a further step is needed to determine whether the product x1(t)x2(t) is real or complex
multiple-choice structure with 4 options indicates comparison across several candidate logical outcomes
verifiability: answer is a discrete textual statement matching one of the provided choices
the underlying reasoning is based on checkable Fourier-transform symmetry rules, so a downstream verifier could assess the chosen option if the figure is available

risk_flags: asset_registry_record issues mention missing choice options, but choices are present in the current input; input/registry inconsistency may affect downstream tooling
image-required reasoning: verification depends on access to and correct interpretation of the figure

decision_reasons: Question, image, and answer are all present, so the sample still retains recoverable core semantics and should not be rejected.
The item appears to be multiple-choice ('Which one of the following statements is TRUE?') but the registry reports missing choice options, creating a material completeness issue.
Initial scoring indicates the reasoning is strongly image-dependent, so downstream verification and reconstruction are riskier despite the available assets.
There is an input/registry inconsistency around whether the choices are available, so the sample should be kept with reduced priority for follow-up.
